title: Mastery of Your Anxiety and Panic_ Workbook
book: Mastery of Your Anxiety and Panic_ Workbook

node_types.description: An individual receiving or providing mental health support, including patients and helpers.

properties.name: role
properties.description: Role of the person (e.g., patient, helper, therapist, family_member)

properties.name: cultural_background
properties.description: Cultural or religious background, including Islamic alignment if relevant

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 10 Involving Others

node_types.description: A physical or psychological symptom experienced during anxiety or panic.

properties.name: name
properties.description: Name of the symptom (e.g., dizziness, heart rate increase)

properties.name: intensity_scale
properties.description: Intensity of the symptom on a 0-10 scale

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 4 Panic Attacks Are Not Harmful
Chapter 6 Breathing Skills
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms

node_types.description: An episode of intense fear or discomfort with abrupt onset and specific symptoms.

properties.name: date_time
properties.description: Date and time of the panic attack

properties.name: expected
properties.description: Whether the attack was expected or unexpected

properties.name: context
properties.description: Context or situation in which the attack occurred

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 4 Panic Attacks Are Not Harmful
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

node_types.description: A specific situation or environment that provokes anxiety or is avoided due to fear of panic.

properties.name: distance_from_home
properties.description: Distance from home in the situation

properties.name: time_of_day
properties.description: Time of day the situation occurs

properties.name: accompanied
properties.description: Whether the person is alone or accompanied

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

node_types.description: A negative or catastrophic thought related to anxiety, panic, or agoraphobia.

properties.name: odds_estimate
properties.description: Estimated odds of the feared event (0-100 scale)

properties.name: thought_error_type
properties.description: Type of thinking error (e.g., catastrophizing, jumping to conclusions)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 4 Panic Attacks Are Not Harmful
Chapter 7 Thinking Skills
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

node_types.description: A skill or technique used to manage anxiety, panic, or agoraphobia.

properties.name: name
properties.description: Name of the coping skill (e.g., breathing, cognitive restructuring)

properties.name: skill_type
properties.description: Type of skill (e.g., breathing, thinking, exposure)

properties.name: instructions
properties.description: Instructions or steps for the skill

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 6 Breathing Skills
Chapter 7 Thinking Skills
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

node_types.description: A behavior, object, or strategy used to prevent or reduce anxiety, often reinforcing avoidance.

properties.name: description
properties.description: Description of the safety behavior or object

properties.name: behavior_type
properties.description: Type (e.g., superstitious object, safety signal, distraction)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

node_types.description: A pharmacological treatment used for anxiety or panic.

properties.name: medication_class
properties.description: Class of medication (e.g., SSRI, SNRI, benzodiazepine, beta-blocker)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 11 Medications

node_types.description: A record of daily mood, anxiety, depression, and worry levels.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

node_types.description: A structured record of treatment progress, including frequency and severity of panic attacks, changes in thoughts, and avoidance behaviors.

properties.name: date_range
properties.description: Time period covered by the record

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

node_types.description: A plan for ongoing practice of coping skills, exposure, and maintenance strategies.

properties.name: revision_frequency
properties.description: How often the plan is revised

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person has experienced a specific panic attack.

properties.name: recorded_by
properties.description: Who recorded the attack (self, helper, therapist)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 4 Panic Attacks Are Not Harmful

edge_types.description: Links a panic attack to the symptoms experienced during the episode.

properties.name: symptom_intensity
properties.description: Intensity of the symptom during the attack

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 4 Panic Attacks Are Not Harmful

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person avoids a specific situation due to anxiety or panic.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

edge_types.description: Indicates that a situation provokes specific symptoms.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person has a specific negative thought related to anxiety or panic.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 7 Thinking Skills
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms

edge_types.description: Indicates that a coping skill is used to challenge or reframe a negative thought.

properties.name: challenge_method
properties.description: Method used to challenge the thought

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 7 Thinking Skills
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person uses a specific coping skill.

properties.name: usage_frequency
properties.description: How often the skill is used

properties.name: context_of_use
properties.description: Context in which the skill is applied

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 3 Negative Cycles of Panic and Agoraphobia
Chapter 6 Breathing Skills
Chapter 7 Thinking Skills
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person uses a safety behavior or object to manage anxiety.

properties.name: frequency
properties.description: How often the safety behavior is used

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person is prescribed or takes a specific medication.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 8 Facing Physical Symptoms
Chapter 9 Facing Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 11 Medications

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person has recorded their mood, anxiety, and depression levels.

properties.name: recording_method
properties.description: Method or form used for recording

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 5 Establishing Your Hierarchy of Agoraphobia Situations
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person has a structured record of their treatment progress.

properties.name: recording_period
properties.description: Time period covered by the progress record

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 2 Learning to Record Panic and Anxiety
Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person has a plan for ongoing practice and maintenance.
supported_by_chapters: Chapter 12 Accomplishments, Maintenance, and Relapse Prevention

edge_types.description: Indicates that a person (patient) is supported by another person (helper, family member, therapist) in their treatment.

properties.name: support_type
properties.description: Type of support provided (e.g., emotional, practical, skill prompting)

supported_by_chapters: Chapter 10 Involving Others
